agent.system_template:
You are an expert software engineer debugging a C/C++ program. You
have a single tool: bash.

Your response must contain exactly ONE bash code block with ONE command
(or commands connected with && or ||). Include a THOUGHT section before
your command where you explain your reasoning process. Format your
response as shown in <format_example>.

<format_example>
Your reasoning and analysis here. Explain why you want to perform the action.

```mswea_bash_command
your_command_here
```
</format_example>

Useful tools available via bash:
  ls, cat, head, tail, grep, find, file, nm, objdump, strings, wc
  ./build/prog [args...]                 — run the buggy binary
  gdb -batch -ex 'run' -ex 'bt' --args ./build/prog [args]   — get a backtrace
  lldb -batch -o 'run' -o 'bt' ./build/prog                  — alternative
  nl -ba <file> | sed -n 'N,Mp'           — read source lines

DO NOT use interactive programs (vim, less, top, etc). Each command runs
in a fresh subshell so `cd` and exported environment variables do not
persist between commands. Use `cd /path && cmd` or `VAR=val cmd` if you
need to chain.

Failure to follow these rules will cause your response to be rejected.
agent.instance_template:
{{task}}

Use bash to investigate. Run the binary, run gdb in batch mode if it
crashes, read source files. After 3-8 steps you should have enough
evidence.

To finish, submit with this exact bash command (and write your
diagnosis BEFORE it, in the same response):

```mswea_bash_command
echo COMPLETE_TASK_AND_SUBMIT_FINAL_OUTPUT
```

Your final response must include three labelled paragraphs in the
THOUGHT before the submit bash command:

ROOT CAUSE: <file:line and what is wrong, in your own words>
LOCAL FIX: <minimal patch>
GLOBAL FIX: <structural change preventing this class of bug>

Reminder: every response must contain exactly one bash code block. If
you write only prose without a bash block, your response is rejected.
Each command runs in a fresh subshell.

<system_information>
{{system}} {{release}} {{machine}}
</system_information>

You are an expert software engineer debugging a C/C++ program. You
have a single tool: bash.

Your response must contain exactly ONE bash code block with ONE command
(or commands connected with && or ||). Include a THOUGHT section before
your command where you explain your reasoning process. Format your
response as shown in <format_example>.

  ./build/prog [args...]                 — run the buggy binary
  gdb -batch -ex 'run' -ex 'bt' --args ./build/prog [args]   — get a backtrace
  lldb -batch -o 'run' -o 'bt' ./build/prog                  — alternative

DO NOT use interactive programs (vim, less, top, etc). Each command runs

persist between commands. Use `cd /path && cmd` or `VAR=val cmd` if you
need to chain.

Failure to follow these rules will cause your response to be rejected.

You're debugging a C/C++ bug. The buggy binary is at `./build/prog` and the source file is `program.c` in the current directory.

Run command: `./build/prog`

Expected behavior: crashes when run (likely a sanitizer report or signal).

Use bash to investigate (run the binary, run gdb in batch mode, read the source). Identify the root cause and propose both a local fix and a structural global fix.


Use bash to investigate. Run the binary, run gdb in batch mode if it
crashes, read source files. After 3-8 steps you should have enough
evidence.

To finish, submit with this exact bash command (and write your
diagnosis BEFORE it, in the same response):

```mswea_bash_command
echo COMPLETE_TASK_AND_SUBMIT_FINAL_OUTPUT
```

Your final response must include three labelled paragraphs in the
THOUGHT before the submit bash command:

ROOT CAUSE: <file:line and what is wrong, in your own words>
LOCAL FIX: <minimal patch>
GLOBAL FIX: <structural change preventing this class of bug>

Reminder: every response must contain exactly one bash code block. If
you write only prose without a bash block, your response is rejected.
Each command runs in a fresh subshell.

<system_information>
Darwin 24.5.0 arm64
</system_information>

```mswea_bash_command
./build/prog
```

messages.content:
The sanitizer already identifies a heap-buffer-overflow in `first_field` at `program.c:17`, with allocation at line 12. I need to inspect the source around those lines (and optionally get a debugger backtrace) to confirm the allocation/copy logic and understand the intended behavior.

```mswea_bash_command
nl -ba program.c | sed -n '1,80p' && gdb -batch -ex 'run' -ex 'bt' --args ./build/prog
```

     1	#include <stdio.h>

     4	
     5	/* Return a freshly allocated copy of the first CSV field in `line`. */
     6	static char *first_field(const char *line) {
     7	    const char *comma = strchr(line, ',');
     8	    size_t n = comma ? (size_t)(comma - line) : strlen(line);
     9	
    10	    /* Allocate a buffer for the field and copy the characters into
    11	     * it, terminating the result. */
    12	    char *out = (char *)malloc(n);
    13	    if (!out) {
    14	        return NULL;
    15	    }
    16	    memcpy(out, line, n);
    17	    out[n] = '\0';     /* terminate the string */
    18	    return out;
    19	}
    20	
    21	int main(void) {
    22	    const char *line = "alice,bob,carol\n";
